prompt: Could the rapeutic interventions aimed at modulating plasma metal ion concentrations represent effective preventive or therapeutic strategies against cardiovascular diseases? What types of interventions—such as supplementation—have been proposed, and is there clinical evidence supporting their feasibility and efficacy?
article:
Yes—but only in **select, biologically “targetable” scenarios** where (a) the measured metal biomarker reliably reflects a modifiable pathophysiologic deficit/toxic exposure, (b) changing it is feasible and safe, and (c) randomized trials show benefit on clinically meaningful outcomes. For most metals, **associations** with cardiovascular disease (CVD) risk are stronger than **interventional proof** that deliberately changing *plasma/serum* concentrations prevents or treats CVD.

Below is what has been proposed, what has been tested, and what the clinical evidence supports as of Feb 2026.

---

## 1) First principles: “plasma metal ions” are rarely free ions, and they’re often poor therapeutic targets unless context is right

### 1.1 What is actually measured
Most “plasma/serum metal” tests quantify **total element concentration**, not the physiologically active “free ion.” Many metals circulate largely **protein-bound**:
- **Copper:** mostly bound to **ceruloplasmin/transcuprein/metallothionein**; <10% is albumin-bound and in equilibrium with amino acids [[67]](https://pediatric.testcatalog.org/show/CUS1).  
- **Selenium:** in healthy plasma/serum, ~**53%** is in **selenoprotein P**, ~**39%** in **glutathione peroxidase**, and ~**9%** in albumin [[72]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8849037/).

Implication: changing a serum number may reflect changes in binding proteins (inflammation, hormones, liver function) rather than a true “bioavailable pool.”

### 1.2 Measurement is technically feasible but preanalytically fragile
Modern multi-element testing commonly uses **ICP‑MS**, which can quantify many elements from small samples and is suitable for biomonitoring/routine workflows [[66]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7239469/). However, reliable measurement requires:
- **Metal-free collection tubes/handling** (trace-element tubes, avoid contamination) [[67]](https://pediatric.testcatalog.org/show/CUS1), [[68]](https://www.mayocliniclabs.com/test-catalog/overview/9765).
- Awareness that contrast agents (gadolinium/iodine/barium) can interfere with ICP‑MS tests; Mayo advises **waiting 96 hours** after such contrast before collection [[67]](https://pediatric.testcatalog.org/show/CUS1), [[68]](https://www.mayocliniclabs.com/test-catalog/overview/9765).

### 1.3 Interpretation is heavily confounded by inflammation and renal function
Two major interpretive pitfalls:
- **Acute-phase/inflammation effects** can raise ceruloplasmin (and serum copper) and can distort interpretation in liver disease and infection/inflammation [[67]](https://pediatric.testcatalog.org/show/CUS1).
- For **iron**, ferritin is an **acute-phase reactant**; inflammation increases hepcidin and traps iron in storage, so ferritin can be normal/high while functional iron deficiency persists [[74]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5878890/). Hence HF trials/guidelines use ferritin + TSAT criteria rather than serum iron alone [[74]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5878890/).

### 1.4 Clinical strategy implication
“Metal modulation” is most plausible when:
1) The biomarker reflects a **causal, reversible constraint** (e.g., **iron deficiency in heart failure**), or  
2) The intervention is **exposure reduction** for a toxic metal with documented cardiovascular harm (e.g., lead/arsenic), rather than attempting to “tune” blood levels pharmacologically.

---

## 2) Essential metals: what interventions exist and what clinical evidence supports CVD prevention/therapy?

### Quick map (evidence strength for CVD outcomes)
- **Strongest therapeutic evidence:** **IV iron** in **heart failure with iron deficiency** (reduces HF hospitalizations; symptom/QoL benefit; mortality effect uncertain) [[15]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12353798/), [[16]](https://clinicaltrialresults.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/FAIR-HF2-Results-ACC-fin-1.pdf), [[17]](https://www.acc.org/Latest-in-Cardiology/Clinical-Trials/2023/08/23/23/46/heart-fid).  
- **Solid evidence in specific acute settings (not chronic prevention):** **magnesium** for torsades/selected arrhythmias and for prevention of postoperative AF (POAF) [[119]](https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/circulationaha.110.970988), [[116]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12285661/).  
- **Mostly neutral or inconsistent on hard outcomes:** selenium (alone), zinc, copper; calcium controversial/possibly harmful; manganese lacks trial evidence [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7433291/), [[12]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37399684/), [[20]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22444781/), [[22]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10111600/), [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11682260/).

---

## 3) Iron: the clearest case where “metal biomarker modulation” is a real cardiovascular therapy

### 3.1 What is being modulated
Not “plasma iron” per se, but **iron deficiency defined by ferritin/TSAT** (because ferritin alone is confounded by inflammation) [[74]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5878890/). HF trials often define iron deficiency as:
- ferritin <100 µg/L, or ferritin 100–299 µg/L with TSAT <20% (commonly used) [[16]](https://clinicaltrialresults.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/FAIR-HF2-Results-ACC-fin-1.pdf), [[17]](https://www.acc.org/Latest-in-Cardiology/Clinical-Trials/2023/08/23/23/46/heart-fid).

### 3.2 Intervention type
Primarily **intravenous iron** (ferric carboxymaltose; ferric derisomaltose) in symptomatic HF with iron deficiency [[15]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12353798/), [[17]](https://www.acc.org/Latest-in-Cardiology/Clinical-Trials/2023/08/23/23/46/heart-fid).

### 3.3 Clinical efficacy
A 2025 systematic review/meta-analysis (6 major HF trials; **7,175** patients) found IV iron reduced:
- Composite of **recurrent HF hospitalizations + cardiovascular mortality** at 12 months: **RR 0.72** (95% CI 0.55–0.89)  
- Same composite over complete follow-up: **RR 0.81** (95% CI 0.63–0.97)  
- Recurrent HF hospitalizations at 12 months: **RR 0.69** (95% CI 0.48–0.88) [[15]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12353798/).

Mortality alone trends favorable but is not definitive:
- CV mortality HR 0.80 (0.61–1.03) at 12 months; HR 0.87 (0.73–1.04) overall [[15]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12353798/).  
- All-cause mortality HR 0.82 (0.65–1.03) at 12 months; HR 0.92 (0.80–1.07) overall [[15]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12353798/).

Trial-specific highlights:
- **FAIR‑HF2** (ferric carboxymaltose correction up to 2,000 mg + maintenance 500 mg q4mo) reported HR **0.71** (0.53–0.94) at 12 months for CV death or first HF hospitalization (as presented) [[16]](https://clinicaltrialresults.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/FAIR-HF2-Results-ACC-fin-1.pdf).  
- **HEART‑FID** (3,065 pts) showed the hierarchical composite was only “marginally improved” (win ratio 1.10; threshold for significance not met), with numerically lower death and HF hospitalizations but not a clear endpoint win [[17]](https://www.acc.org/Latest-in-Cardiology/Clinical-Trials/2023/08/23/23/46/heart-fid).

### 3.4 Feasibility and implementation status
- **Guidelines:** AHA/ACC/HFSA HF guidance includes IV iron as reasonable to improve functional status/QoL in iron-deficient HFrEF; ESC HF update includes iron-deficiency recommendations (documented presence) [[81]](https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/full/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001062), [[82]](https://advancesinheartfailure.pt/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/2-2023-Heart-Failure-ESC-Guidelines-update.pdf), [[75]](https://knowidinhf.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/07_Tx_of_ID_in_patients_with_HF_092923_copy.pdf).  
- **Regulatory:** In the US, ferric carboxymaltose (Injectafer) gained an HF-related indication to improve exercise capacity in NYHA II/III HF patients with iron deficiency (approval history and supporting trial summary) [[83]](https://www.drugs.com/history/injectafer.html), [[84]](https://www.empr.com/home/news/injectafer-approved-for-iron-deficiency-in-adults-with-heart-failure/).  
- **Safety:** Meta-analysis findings were consistent with overall safety signals (no clear mortality harm) [[15]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12353798/); trial summaries show similar adverse event rates (e.g., HEART‑FID) [[17]](https://www.acc.org/Latest-in-Cardiology/Clinical-Trials/2023/08/23/23/46/heart-fid). Hypophosphatemia is a recognized adverse effect for ferric carboxymaltose (reported in FDA-related clinical summaries) [[84]](https://www.empr.com/home/news/injectafer-approved-for-iron-deficiency-in-adults-with-heart-failure/).

**Bottom line:** *Yes*—modulating iron status is an evidence-based therapeutic strategy in HF with iron deficiency, mainly improving symptoms and reducing HF hospitalizations, with uncertain mortality benefit [[15]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12353798/), [[17]](https://www.acc.org/Latest-in-Cardiology/Clinical-Trials/2023/08/23/23/46/heart-fid).

---

## 4) Magnesium: useful for specific arrhythmia contexts and modest BP lowering; not proven for preventing MI/stroke

### 4.1 Intervention types proposed
1) **IV magnesium sulfate** for acute electrophysiologic indications (torsades, rapid AF adjunct, perioperative arrhythmia prevention).  
2) **Oral magnesium supplementation** (various salts) for chronic BP/metabolic risk modification, and exploratory arrhythmia prevention.

### 4.2 Arrhythmias: strongest evidence is situational (acute/perioperative), not long-term prevention
**Post-cardiac surgery POAF prevention:**  
A 2025 meta-analysis of **24 RCTs (3,373 participants)** found magnesium reduced POAF risk (RR **0.55**, 95% CI 0.41–0.74), with stronger certainty in trials with lower risk of bias (RR **0.70**, 0.58–0.84). No mortality effect (RR 1.00, wide CI) [[116]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12285661/).

**Adjunct in rapid atrial fibrillation (nonoperative acute care):**  
A meta-analysis of 6 RCTs (745 patients) found IV magnesium added to standard care improved:
- Rate control: 63% vs 40% (OR **2.49**, 1.80–3.45)  
- Conversion to sinus rhythm: 21% vs 14% (OR **1.75**, 1.08–2.84)  
Flushing increased (9% vs 0.4%) [[4]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34162502/).

**After coronary revascularization / ACS settings:**  
A 2018 meta-analysis (22 studies; 6,061 pts) reported lower ventricular and supraventricular arrhythmias with magnesium vs placebo (OR **0.32** and **0.42**, respectively), but the literature has heterogeneity and quality concerns [[3]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6025730/).

**Guideline-level acute indication (torsades):**  
AHA ACLS guidance (2010) stated magnesium sulfate should be considered **only** for torsades with long QT (Class IIb) [[119]](https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/circulationaha.110.970988).

### 4.3 Acute MI: IV magnesium is not supported in modern reperfusion-era practice
A Cochrane review on IV magnesium in suspected AMI found overall evidence does **not** support continued routine use; a very large well-designed trial contradicted earlier smaller trials, with concerns about heterogeneity and publication bias; magnesium increased hypotension and bradycardia while reducing some arrhythmic outcomes [[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8407081/).  
A randomized trial of IV magnesium around primary angioplasty showed no improvement in myocardial damage or short-term outcomes [[6]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/11099993/).

### 4.4 Blood pressure: modest average reductions; larger effects when deficient/hypertensive
A 2025 meta-analysis of 38 RCTs (2,709 participants; elemental Mg 82–637 mg/day; median 365 mg; median 12 weeks) found:
- SBP: −**2.81** mmHg (95% CI −4.32 to −1.29)  
- DBP: −**2.05** mmHg (95% CI −3.23 to −0.88)  
with larger effects in hypomagnesemia and in treated hypertensives [[110]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12529988/).

### 4.5 Chronic “secondary prevention” after MI: one RCT suggested possible harm
A 1-year randomized trial in 468 AMI survivors using 15 mmol/day magnesium hydroxide found no reduction in reinfarction/sudden death/CABG and a **higher** event rate in combined outcomes (RR **1.55**, 1.07–2.25) [[103]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8401013/).

### 4.6 Safety and feasibility
- In CKD, an 8-week RCT in CKD stages 3–4 (slow-release magnesium hydroxide 15 or 30 mmol/day; baseline Mg <0.82 mmol/L) found supplementation was safe/tolerated and improved a calcification propensity measure (T50) without serious adverse events, but no clinical endpoints were studied [[129]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5678662/).  
- Real-world risk: severe hypermagnesemia can occur in elderly patients with renal dysfunction using magnesium oxide laxatives/antacids; a case series included a death and recommends monitoring high-risk patients [[130]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6361089/).

**Bottom line:** Magnesium modulation is **clinically effective for certain arrhythmia-related uses and modestly lowers BP**, but it is **not established** as a general preventive strategy against MI/stroke, and at least one post-MI oral trial suggested possible harm [[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8407081/), [[103]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8401013/), [[110]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12529988/).

---

## 5) Selenium: divergent stories—null (and possible harm) in selenium-replete populations vs hypothesis-generating benefit in low-selenium elderly when combined with CoQ10

### 5.1 Selenium-only supplementation for primary CVD prevention: no benefit; safety concerns
A Cochrane review of selenium-only supplementation trials (12 trials; 19,715 participants; largest from SELECT and NPC) found no significant effect on:
- all-cause mortality (RR 0.97)  
- CVD mortality (RR 0.97)  
- non-fatal CVD events (RR 0.96)  
- all CVD events (RR 1.03) [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7433291/).

Safety signals:
- Possible increased type 2 diabetes risk (overall RR 1.06; not statistically significant in pooled analysis) [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7433291/).  
- SELECT reported alopecia and dermatitis increases [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7433291/).  
- NPC secondary analyses found increased diabetes risk particularly in those with high baseline selenium; a BMJ summary reported HR **1.55** (1.03–2.33) for diabetes with 200 µg/day selenium and a stronger risk in highest baseline selenium strata (reported as 2.7-fold) [[143]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1971213/).  
- The Linus Pauling Institute summary notes SELECT was stopped for lack of benefit and diabetes concern; NPC saw diabetes risk increase in highest baseline selenium tertile [[7]](https://lpi.oregonstate.edu/mic/minerals/selenium).

### 5.2 Combined selenium + CoQ10 (KiSel‑10): reduced cardiovascular mortality in a low-selenium Swedish elderly cohort—but small, with major attrition, and not widely replicated
The Swedish KiSel‑10 double-blind RCT randomized **443** elderly community-living participants to **selenium yeast 200 µg/day + CoQ10 200 mg/day** vs placebo for 48 months.
- At ~5.2 years follow-up: CV mortality **5.9% vs 12.6%** (P=0.015), lower NT‑proBNP, better echo score [[133]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167527312005931).  
- 10-year follow-up: adjusted CV mortality HR **0.51** (0.36–0.74) [[131]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4666408/).  
- 12-year follow-up: adjusted CV mortality HR **0.59** (0.42–0.81) [[132]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5894963/).

However, the investigators repeatedly emphasize limitations: small trial, restricted population, and substantial withdrawals; in the 10-year report, only 228 completed the intervention and 29.1% did not complete, often citing too many tablets [[131]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4666408/). These findings are best treated as **hypothesis-generating** despite impressive hazard ratios [[133]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167527312005931), [[131]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4666408/), [[132]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5894963/).

### 5.3 Dose matters; higher-dose selenium may be harmful even in moderately low-selenium regions
The Danish PRECISE study (491 elderly) randomized to 100/200/300 µg/day selenium-enriched yeast vs placebo for 5 years, with extended follow-up. All-cause mortality was **higher** in the 300 µg/day arm over full follow-up (HR **1.59**, 1.02–2.46) [[139]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0891584918300704). This supports a **non-linear (possibly U-shaped) risk** and cautions against high-dose supplementation absent clear deficiency [[139]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0891584918300704).

### 5.4 Heart failure and deficiency-targeted selenium: limited evidence; trials ongoing
- In selenium-deficient peripartum cardiomyopathy (Nigeria), an open-label randomized trial (selenium 200 µg/day for 3 months vs nothing) did **not** improve the primary composite but reduced HF symptoms and showed a non-significant mortality trend [[140]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12872-020-01739-z).  
- Larger pragmatic endpoint trials of selenium+CoQ10 in HF are underway (e.g., SELEQT‑HF) [[142]](https://www.centerwatch.com/clinical-trials/listings/NCT07234422/the-seleqt-hf-selenium-and-coq10-nutrition-for-heart-failure-study-will-evaluate-the-effect-of-adding-selenium-and-coq10-on-top-of-standard-hf-therapy-in-patients-with-hf-and-will-do-so-using-a-pragmatic-registry-based-randomized-controlled-trial-in-the-netherlands).

**Bottom line:** Selenium supplementation **is not supported** for general CVD prevention in selenium-replete populations and may increase diabetes risk in some groups [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7433291/), [[143]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1971213/). Targeted use in low-selenium settings—especially combined with CoQ10—has intriguing but not definitive endpoint evidence (KiSel‑10) and needs replication [[133]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167527312005931), [[132]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5894963/), [[142]](https://www.centerwatch.com/clinical-trials/listings/NCT07234422/the-seleqt-hf-selenium-and-coq10-nutrition-for-heart-failure-study-will-evaluate-the-effect-of-adding-selenium-and-coq10-on-top-of-standard-hf-therapy-in-patients-with-hf-and-will-do-so-using-a-pragmatic-registry-based-randomized-controlled-trial-in-the-netherlands).

---

## 6) Zinc and copper: plausible biology, but supplementation has little direct CVD endpoint evidence

### 6.1 Zinc supplementation
Evidence in the retrieved corpus is largely on **risk factors**, not hard CVD outcomes:
- Meta-analyses report mixed lipid effects (e.g., modest LDL reductions in some analyses) [[11]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1186/s12986-015-0023-4).  
- A 2023 dose-response meta-analysis of RCTs reported improvements in several metabolic/inflammatory biomarkers (TG, total cholesterol, glycemic indices, CRP, IL-6, TNF‑α, oxidative stress markers), but no noticeable effect on SBP/DBP or LDL/HDL in pooled analyses as reported [[12]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37399684/).  
No strong RCT evidence of reduced MI/stroke mortality is established in these sources.

### 6.2 Copper supplementation
A small randomized trial (healthy adults with moderately high cholesterol) using **copper 2 mg/day** for 8 weeks increased copper enzyme activities but did **not** significantly change CRP, homocysteine, or total/LDL/HDL cholesterol; oxidized LDL reduction was inconsistent [[20]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22444781/).  
Copper testing/interpretation is complicated by binding and acute-phase shifts (ceruloplasmin changes with inflammation, pregnancy, estrogen) [[67]](https://pediatric.testcatalog.org/show/CUS1).

**Bottom line:** Zinc/copper modulation remains **investigational** for CVD prevention/therapy; evidence is mainly surrogate-based and not sufficient for routine CVD strategies [[12]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37399684/), [[20]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22444781/).

---

## 7) Calcium and manganese: calcium supplementation is controversial for CVD; manganese lacks interventional evidence

### 7.1 Calcium supplementation and CVD outcomes
A 2023 collaborative meta-analysis of placebo-controlled RCTs found **no statistically significant** increased risk of MI, CHD death, stroke, or all-cause mortality with calcium alone (~1 g/day) or calcium + vitamin D [[22]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10111600/). However, the point estimate for MI with calcium alone was >1 (RR 1.15) with wide CI, leaving uncertainty for smaller harms [[22]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10111600/).

Secondary reviews remain concerned:
- A 2025 safety review argues calcium supplements may increase MI risk by ~10–20% and highlights physiology: calcium “bolus” raises circulating calcium for hours, with acute increases in coagulability and calcification propensity; kidney stone risk increases; dietary calcium appears safer than supplements [[24]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11914-025-00904-7).  
- A 2026 cardiovascular review states no consistent RCT signal for improved outcomes; vitamin D appears neutral; there is “some evidence” calcium alone may increase CHD risk; routine supplementation is not supported for cardiovascular health [[25]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1050173825001045).

### 7.2 Calcium as a “metal-modulating” adjunct for toxic metals (lead)
Calcium can reduce circulating lead (likely via reduced bone resorption and/or reduced absorption):
- In lactating women, a double-blind RCT of **1,200 mg/day calcium carbonate** showed a modest blood lead reduction overall and larger reductions in women with higher bone lead and good compliance [[152]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12606887/). CDC summarizes related trials as showing ~20% lower maternal blood lead vs placebo in pregnancy/lactation contexts [[151]](https://www.cdc.gov/lead-prevention/media/pdfs/2025/03/cdc_147837_DS1.pdf).  
This is a **toxic-metal** modulation rationale, not a direct CVD strategy.

### 7.3 Manganese
Evidence is observational with possible **U-shaped** associations between blood manganese and CVD prevalence [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11682260/), and cohort/meta-analysis signals for diabetes risk are inconsistent [[26]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1279770725002799). No strong RCT supplementation evidence for CVD prevention/treatment is established in the provided sources.

**Bottom line:** Calcium supplementation should not be viewed as a CVD prevention tool; its role may be limited to bone/deficiency indications and specific lead-exposure contexts [[22]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10111600/), [[24]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11914-025-00904-7), [[152]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12606887/). Manganese supplementation for CVD lacks trial support [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11682260/).

---

## 8) Toxic metals: reducing exposure is likely cardiovascular-preventive; chelation as “therapy” is not convincingly effective for CVD

Toxic metals (lead, cadmium, arsenic, mercury) have strong epidemiologic links to hypertension/CVD risk, and **population-level exposure reductions** likely yield cardiovascular benefit. But translating this into individual-level “plasma metal lowering” therapy is challenging.

---

## 9) Lead: exposure reduction is the primary strategy; EDTA chelation lowered lead but did not reliably reduce CVD events

### 9.1 Upstream prevention: global burden and nutrition interactions
WHO states lead exposure caused **>1.5 million deaths in 2021**, primarily via cardiovascular effects, and there is **no known safe exposure level** [[150]](https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/lead-poisoning-and-health). WHO also notes calcium/iron deficiency increases lead absorption, especially in malnutrition [[150]](https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/lead-poisoning-and-health).

### 9.2 Longitudinal human evidence linking declining lead to improved BP
In the Strong Heart Family Study, individuals with larger declines in whole-blood lead over ~10 years had greater SBP reductions; highest vs lowest tertile of lead decline was associated with ~**−7 mmHg** difference in systolic BP change [[154]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10926826/). This supports cardiovascular relevance of reducing internal lead dose, even at relatively low baseline levels [[154]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10926826/).

### 9.3 Chelation therapy (EDTA) as a CVD treatment: mixed trial history, negative replication
**TACT (original NIH trial; post-MI, not selected for high lead):**  
- 1,708 stable post-MI patients randomized to 40 EDTA-based infusions vs placebo (plus factorial oral vitamins). Primary composite endpoint reduced modestly (HR **0.82**, 0.69–0.99; P=0.035) [[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4066975/), [[37]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23532240/). Authors concluded results were insufficient for routine use [[37]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23532240/).

**TACT diabetes subgroup (prespecified):**  
- In 633 diabetics, large benefit reported (HR **0.59**, 0.44–0.79) for the composite; interaction by diabetes status reported [[38]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24254885/). This drove interest in replication [[38]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24254885/).

**TACT2 (replication in diabetics post-MI):**  
- 959 diabetic post-MI participants; EDTA chelation **lowered blood lead ~61%** (median ~9 → ~3.5 µg/L), showing biomarker modulation feasibility [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11325247/), [[35]](https://www.niehs.nih.gov/news/factor/2024/9/papers/chelation).  
- But **no reduction** in primary composite endpoint (adjusted HR **0.93**, 0.76–1.16; P=0.53) [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11325247/), [[86]](https://www.acc.org/Latest-in-Cardiology/Articles/2024/04/02/17/02/sun-945am-tact2-acc-2024).  
NIEHS concluded TACT2 did **not** support EDTA chelation to reduce CVD risk in this population [[35]](https://www.niehs.nih.gov/news/factor/2024/9/papers/chelation).

**Safety/feasibility constraints:**  
Chelation is resource-intensive (weeks-months of infusions) and not FDA-approved for coronary disease [[36]](https://www.nccih.nih.gov/health/chelation-for-coronary-heart-disease-what-you-need-to-know). Serious risks include hypocalcemia and kidney damage [[36]](https://www.nccih.nih.gov/health/chelation-for-coronary-heart-disease-what-you-need-to-know). In TACT/TACT2, serious adverse event rates were not higher than placebo in summaries, but the regimen is burdensome and not proven effective after TACT2 [[36]](https://www.nccih.nih.gov/health/chelation-for-coronary-heart-disease-what-you-need-to-know), [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11325247/).

**Bottom line:** Lowering blood lead is a compelling prevention target; however, **EDTA chelation is not an evidence-based CVD therapy** after the negative TACT2 replication, despite successfully lowering blood lead [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11325247/), [[35]](https://www.niehs.nih.gov/news/factor/2024/9/papers/chelation).

---

## 10) Arsenic: water-source mitigation reduces internal dose and is linked to lower cardiovascular mortality

### 10.1 Practical interventions
Well testing, labeling unsafe wells, education, and well switching/drilling deeper wells have been studied.

A cluster randomized trial in Bangladesh found arsenic testing + education led many households using contaminated wells to switch sources; switching was accompanied by significant reductions in urinary arsenic [[160]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3506475/).

### 10.2 Evidence linking arsenic reduction to lower CVD mortality
A large prospective Bangladesh cohort analysis reported that **reductions in urinary arsenic** were associated with lower CVD mortality; each IQR decrease in urinary arsenic was associated with lower CVD mortality (aHR **0.77**, 0.73–0.81), and participants whose arsenic fell below the median had markedly lower CVD mortality compared with persistently high exposure (aHR **0.43**, 0.34–0.53) [[47]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41247717/). (A PDF excerpt provides similar categorical pattern results) [[48]](https://bookcafe.yuntsg.com/ueditor/jsp/upload/file/20260210/1770688502824007958.pdf).

**Bottom line:** Among toxic metals, arsenic has unusually strong evidence that **exposure reduction → lower cardiovascular mortality**, albeit from observational change analyses rather than randomized mortality trials [[47]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41247717/), [[160]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3506475/).

---

## 11) Cadmium: biggest “intervention lever” is tobacco control/smoking cessation; chelation evidence for CVD benefit is lacking

### 11.1 Practical interventions (strong public health guidance)
CDC/ATSDR advises:
- **Stop smoking** (cadmium in tobacco smoke; lung absorption)  
- Ensure **adequate iron** because iron deficiency increases cadmium absorption  
- Occupational hygiene/ventilation/PPE and avoid further exposure [[155]](https://archive.cdc.gov/www_atsdr_cdc_gov/csem/cadmium/Instructions.html), [[156]](https://archive.cdc.gov/www_atsdr_cdc_gov/csem/cadmium/Treatment.html).

### 11.2 CVD linkage and policy relevance
- NHANES prospective analysis: blood/urine cadmium associated with increased CVD and heart disease mortality even at low exposure levels [[158]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3404657/).  
- A Malmö cohort mediation analysis suggests a substantial fraction of smoking-induced ischemic heart disease risk is mediated through cadmium exposure [[157]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6570857/).  
- US urine cadmium levels declined over 1988–2008; declines partly attributed to reduced smoking and smoke exposure, implying tobacco control reduces cadmium body burden [[159]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3279452/).

**Bottom line:** Cadmium-lowering for CVD is best approached via **exposure prevention**, especially smoking cessation, rather than pharmacologic lowering [[155]](https://archive.cdc.gov/www_atsdr_cdc_gov/csem/cadmium/Instructions.html), [[157]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6570857/).

---

## 12) Mercury: interventions focus on dietary source management (fish advisories), not “lowering blood mercury” as a CVD therapy

FDA/EPA guidance emphasizes choosing fish lower in methylmercury and following local advisories; FDA notes methylmercury cannot be removed from seafood, so mitigation is dietary choice and regulation/monitoring [[165]](https://www.epa.gov/mercury/guidelines-eating-fish-contain-mercury), [[166]](https://www.fda.gov/food/environmental-contaminants-food/mercury-food). This is primarily framed for neurodevelopmental protection, and robust RCT evidence that lowering mercury improves CVD outcomes is not established in the provided sources.

---

## 13) So—can modulating plasma metal concentrations be an effective CVD preventive/therapeutic strategy?

### 13.1 Where the answer is clearly “yes, in practice”
1) **Heart failure with iron deficiency → IV iron** is a feasible therapy with meaningful clinical benefit (HF hospitalizations, symptoms/QoL; mortality uncertain) [[15]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12353798/), [[16]](https://clinicaltrialresults.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/FAIR-HF2-Results-ACC-fin-1.pdf), [[83]](https://www.drugs.com/history/injectafer.html), [[84]](https://www.empr.com/home/news/injectafer-approved-for-iron-deficiency-in-adults-with-heart-failure/).  
2) **Specific arrhythmia contexts → magnesium (IV)** (torsades; perioperative AF prevention; adjunct for rapid AF) [[119]](https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/circulationaha.110.970988), [[116]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12285661/), [[4]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34162502/).

### 13.2 Where the answer is “plausible but not proven / highly context-dependent”
- **Selenium + CoQ10** in low-selenium elderly: impressive CV mortality reductions in KiSel‑10 but single-center/small with attrition and needs replication; high-dose selenium alone may increase long-term mortality [[133]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167527312005931), [[132]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC5894963/), [[139]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0891584918300704).  
- **Magnesium for chronic CVD prevention:** modest BP reductions; no hard outcome benefit established; at least one post-MI oral trial suggested harm [[110]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12529988/), [[103]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8401013/).  
- **Zinc/copper:** risk-factor changes without hard outcomes; routine CVD use not supported [[12]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37399684/), [[20]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22444781/).

### 13.3 Where the answer is “no as a medical therapy, but yes as population prevention”
- **Lead/arsenic/cadmium:** Reducing exposure/internal dose is strongly justified for cardiovascular prevention, but this is primarily **public health/environmental** intervention rather than clinic-based “ion tuning.” Lead chelation lowered lead but did not reduce events in TACT2 [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11325247/), [[47]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41247717/), [[155]](https://archive.cdc.gov/www_atsdr_cdc_gov/csem/cadmium/Instructions.html).

---

## 14) Types of interventions proposed (organized) and clinical evidence for feasibility/efficacy

### A) Supplementation / replacement (increase essential metals)
- **IV iron (HF + iron deficiency):** strong RCT program; reduced HF hospitalizations; FDA HF indication (US) [[15]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12353798/), [[83]](https://www.drugs.com/history/injectafer.html).  
- **Oral magnesium:** modest BP reductions in meta-analysis; mixed/negative hard outcomes; GI side effects; caution in CKD [[110]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12529988/), [[103]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/8401013/), [[130]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6361089/).  
- **IV magnesium:** beneficial for POAF prevention and rapid AF adjunct; hypotension/bradycardia risks in AMI trials; torsades indication supported in ACLS [[116]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12285661/), [[4]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34162502/), [[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8407081/), [[119]](https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/circulationaha.110.970988).  
- **Selenium:** selenium-only supplementation does not prevent CVD in RCTs and may increase diabetes risk; combined with CoQ10 shows potential in low-selenium elderly but needs replication; avoid high-dose selenium (e.g., 300 µg/day signal) [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7433291/), [[133]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167527312005931), [[139]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0891584918300704).  
- **Zinc/copper:** surrogate improvements (zinc) and mostly null (copper) without endpoint proof [[12]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37399684/), [[20]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/22444781/).

### B) Fortification / food-based mineral delivery
- **Mineral-enriched salt (K/Mg):** in post-stroke patients, a K/Mg salt improved odds of good neurologic recovery vs regular salt (functional outcome, not incident CVD prevention) [[105]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0002916522026636).  
- **Population dietary guidance (e.g., potassium):** WHO recommends higher potassium intake to reduce BP and stroke risk; this is dietary mineral policy rather than plasma targeting [[97]](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK132458/).

### C) Chelation (decrease toxic metals)
- **EDTA chelation:**  
  - TACT: modest composite endpoint benefit post-MI [[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4066975/)  
  - TACT2: no clinical benefit despite 61% blood lead reduction [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11325247/), [[35]](https://www.niehs.nih.gov/news/factor/2024/9/papers/chelation)  
  - Not FDA-approved for CHD; burdensome infusion series; risk of serious adverse events [[36]](https://www.nccih.nih.gov/health/chelation-for-coronary-heart-disease-what-you-need-to-know).  
Overall: not clinically supported for CVD prevention/therapy after replication failure [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11325247/).

### D) Nutritional antagonism (reduce absorption/mobilization of toxic metals)
- **Calcium supplementation in pregnancy/lactation** can modestly reduce blood lead, especially in those with higher bone lead burden and good compliance [[152]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/12606887/), [[151]](https://www.cdc.gov/lead-prevention/media/pdfs/2025/03/cdc_147837_DS1.pdf).  
- **Correct iron deficiency** to reduce lead/cadmium absorption (mechanistically and by public health guidance), but universal high-dose use purely for metal lowering is not established [[151]](https://www.cdc.gov/lead-prevention/media/pdfs/2025/03/cdc_147837_DS1.pdf), [[155]](https://archive.cdc.gov/www_atsdr_cdc_gov/csem/cadmium/Instructions.html).

### E) Exposure-source intervention (the main “toxic metal therapy”)
- **Arsenic:** testing/labeling/well switching reduces urinary arsenic; reductions in urinary arsenic are associated with lower CVD mortality [[160]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3506475/), [[47]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41247717/).  
- **Cadmium:** smoking cessation + occupational controls are the core evidence-backed interventions [[155]](https://archive.cdc.gov/www_atsdr_cdc_gov/csem/cadmium/Instructions.html), [[156]](https://archive.cdc.gov/www_atsdr_cdc_gov/csem/cadmium/Treatment.html).  
- **Lead:** broad upstream controls; within-person declines in blood lead associated with SBP improvements [[154]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10926826/).  
- **Mercury:** fish choice/advisories; cannot remove methylmercury from seafood [[166]](https://www.fda.gov/food/environmental-contaminants-food/mercury-food).

---

## 15) Current “consensus” posture (what clinicians/policymakers effectively recommend)

### 15.1 General supplementation for CVD prevention: not recommended routinely
USPSTF concludes evidence is insufficient for most vitamin/mineral supplements for preventing CVD/cancer (and recommends against vitamin E and beta-carotene specifically) [[76]](https://www.uspreventiveservicestaskforce.org/uspstf/announcements/final-recommendation-statement-vitamin-and-mineral-supplementation-prevent-cvd-and-cancer), [[77]](https://www.uspreventiveservicestaskforce.org/home/getfilebytoken/KprBMtobbwaeMEqZzxwHCz). ACC summaries of large supplement meta-analyses similarly report no meaningful CVD outcome benefit for common minerals including magnesium, selenium, zinc, iron in general populations [[80]](https://www.acc.org/latest-in-cardiology/ten-points-to-remember/2018/05/30/13/47/vitamins-and-minerals-for-cvd). ESC prevention guidance emphasizes dietary patterns and is generally skeptical about supplements for prevention [[79]](https://scc.org.co/wp-content/uploads/2018/10/Guias-prevencion-cardiovascular-2021.pdf).

### 15.2 Clear guideline integration for iron in HF (and increasing regulatory support)
HF guidelines incorporate IV iron replacement in iron-deficient symptomatic HF to improve function/QoL, and ESC HF updates include iron deficiency management recommendations (documented) [[81]](https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/full/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001062), [[82]](https://advancesinheartfailure.pt/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/2-2023-Heart-Failure-ESC-Guidelines-update.pdf), [[75]](https://knowidinhf.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/10/07_Tx_of_ID_in_patients_with_HF_092923_copy.pdf). US FDA labeling supports ferric carboxymaltose to improve exercise capacity in HF with iron deficiency [[83]](https://www.drugs.com/history/injectafer.html), [[84]](https://www.empr.com/home/news/injectafer-approved-for-iron-deficiency-in-adults-with-heart-failure/).

### 15.3 Toxic metal reduction: framed as public health rather than cardiology pharmacotherapy
WHO emphasizes prevention and exposure elimination for lead, with huge cardiovascular mortality burden [[150]](https://www.who.int/news-room/fact-sheets/detail/lead-poisoning-and-health). Chelation is not endorsed as a standard CVD therapy after TACT2 [[35]](https://www.niehs.nih.gov/news/factor/2024/9/papers/chelation).

---

## 16) What remains unresolved / high-value next steps
1) **Precision targeting by baseline status**: benefits likely concentrate in deficient/high-exposure strata (iron deficiency; low selenium regions; hypomagnesemia; high lead/arsenic exposure). KiSel‑10 vs SELECT/NPC illustrates this tension [[133]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0167527312005931), [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7433291/).  
2) **Hard endpoints vs surrogates**: many metal interventions improve BP/biomarkers but lack MI/stroke outcome trials (magnesium, zinc).  
3) **Multi-metal interactions / U-shaped curves**: selenium and possibly manganese show non-linear risk patterns, implying “more is not better” and raising the risk of harm from indiscriminate supplementation [[139]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0891584918300704), [[145]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/cardiovascular-medicine/articles/10.3389/fcvm.2021.671618/full), [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11682260/).  
4) **Implementation science**: even when effective, interventions can be burdensome (KiSel‑10 pill burden attrition [[131]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC4666408/); chelation infusion burden [[36]](https://www.nccih.nih.gov/health/chelation-for-coronary-heart-disease-what-you-need-to-know)) or require infrastructure (IV iron services).

---

### Overall conclusion
Therapeutic interventions that modulate metal biomarkers **can** be effective cardiovascular strategies, but the evidence is **highly metal- and context-specific**:

- **Proven therapeutic strategy:** **IV iron** for **heart failure with iron deficiency** (reduces HF hospitalizations; improves symptoms/exercise capacity) [[15]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12353798/), [[83]](https://www.drugs.com/history/injectafer.html).  
- **Effective in targeted acute settings:** **magnesium (IV)** for torsades and some AF/postoperative arrhythmia contexts; modest BP effects with oral magnesium but not proven for hard outcomes [[119]](https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/circulationaha.110.970988), [[116]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12285661/), [[110]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12529988/).  
- **Not supported for routine CVD prevention:** selenium-only supplements, zinc/copper supplements, calcium supplementation (cardiovascular neutrality/possible harm), manganese supplementation [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC7433291/), [[12]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/37399684/), [[22]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10111600/), [[27]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11682260/).  
- **Most effective “toxic metal” approach is upstream exposure reduction** (arsenic/lead/cadmium), not chelation as CVD therapy—especially given **TACT2’s negative clinical outcomes despite major lead lowering** [[30]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11325247/), [[47]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/41247717/), [[155]](https://archive.cdc.gov/www_atsdr_cdc_gov/csem/cadmium/Instructions.html).

If you want, I can end with a “decision framework” (who to test, which biomarker to use, when to intervene, and what to avoid) strictly based on the evidence above.
